examples.explanation: Start: index=0, last=-inf, length=0. At index=0 (3): include (3 > -inf) → recurse with last=3, length=1; skip → recurse with last=-inf, length=0. Include path: index=1 (10): include (10 > 3) → last=10, length=2; skip → last=3, length=1. Include from index=1: index=2 (2): include (2 > 10? no) → skip only; skip → last=10, length=2. Skip from index=1: index=2 (2): include (2 > 3? no) → skip only; skip → last=3, length=1. Continue recursively; max found: path 3→10→20 gives length 3.

Start: index=0, last=-inf, length=0. At index=0 (50): include → last=50, length=1; skip → last=-inf, length=0. Explore include: index=1 (3): include (3 > 50? no) → skip only; skip → last=50, length=1. Skip from start: index=1 (3): include → last=3, length=1; skip → last=-inf, length=0. Recursively, paths like 3→10→40→80 yield length 4, which is max.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible subsequences recursively, checking if each is increasing and tracking the longest length. This approach explores every combination but becomes impractical for larger inputs due to exponential growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for LIS
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution generating all subsequences to find the longest increasing one.
ladders.explanation: The brute force recursion approach solves LIS by exploring every possible subsequence of the input array. The intuition is that for each element, we have two choices: include it in the current subsequence (if it maintains increasing order) or skip it. By recursively exploring both choices, we generate all 2^n possible subsequences. The algorithm maintains the last included element's value to ensure increasing order. At each recursive call, if we include the current element (when it's greater than the last included), we extend the subsequence length by 1; otherwise, we skip it. The base case occurs when we've processed all elements, returning the accumulated length. The maximum length across all recursive paths gives the LIS length. Time complexity is O(2^n) due to exploring all subsequences, and space is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L1 as the complete implementation of the recursive helper. Common mistakes include not properly tracking the last included value, leading to incorrect increasing checks, or missing the skip case, which reduces exploration. Real code pattern: define a recursive function that takes current index and last value, returns max length from include/skip choices.

examples.trace: Start: index=0, last=-inf, length=0. At index=0 (3): include (3 > -inf) → recurse with last=3, length=1; skip → recurse with last=-inf, length=0. Include path: index=1 (10): include (10 > 3) → last=10, length=2; skip → last=3, length=1. Include from index=1: index=2 (2): include (2 > 10? no) → skip only; skip → last=10, length=2. Skip from index=1: index=2 (2): include (2 > 3? no) → skip only; skip → last=3, length=1. Continue recursively; max found: path 3→10→20 gives length 3.

Start: index=0, last=-inf, length=0. At index=0 (50): include → last=50, length=1; skip → last=-inf, length=0. Explore include: index=1 (3): include (3 > 50? no) → skip only; skip → last=50, length=1. Skip from start: index=1 (3): include → last=3, length=1; skip → last=-inf, length=0. Recursively, paths like 3→10→40→80 yield length 4, which is max.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Helper Function
ladders.desc: Recursive function to find LIS length from a given starting point.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that computes the LIS length starting from a specific index with a given last included value. The intuition is to break the problem into smaller subproblems: at each index, decide whether to include the current element (if it increases the sequence) or skip it, and recursively solve for the remaining array. The function takes parameters: current index i, last value last (initialized to negative infinity), and returns the maximum LIS length from i to end. Algorithm: if i equals array length, return 0 (base case). Otherwise, compute includeLength = 0; if arr[i] > last, includeLength = 1 + recurse(i+1, arr[i]). Compute skipLength = recurse(i+1, last). Return max(includeLength, skipLength). Time: O(2^n) as it explores two branches per element; space: O(n) recursion stack. This feeds into L0 as the main recursive logic. Common mistakes: forgetting to pass last value correctly, leading to non-increasing sequences; or not handling base case. Real code pattern: def lis_helper(i, last): if i==n: return 0; include=0; if arr[i]>last: include=1+lis_helper(i+1, arr[i]); skip=lis_helper(i+1, last); return max(include, skip).

examples.trace: i=0, last=-inf. arr[0]=3 > -inf, so includeLength = 1 + recurse(i=1, last=3). Recurse: i=1, last=3. arr[1]=2 > 3? no, so includeLength not set; skipLength = recurse(i=2, last=3). Base: i=2==n, return 0. So skipLength=0, max(0,0)=0. Back: includeLength=1+0=1; skipLength = recurse(i=1, last=-inf): i=1, last=-inf, arr[1]=2 > -inf, include=1+recurse(i=2, last=2)=1+0=1; skip=recurse(i=2, last=-inf)=0; max=1. Return max(1,1)=1.

i=0, last=-inf. arr[0]=1 > -inf, include=1+recurse(1,1). Recurse: i=1, last=1, arr[1]=2>1, include=1+recurse(2,2); i=2, last=2, arr[2]=3>2, include=1+recurse(3,3); base i=3 return 0, so include=1+0=1; skip=recurse(3,2)=0; max=1. Back: include=1+1=2; skip=recurse(2,1): i=2, last=1, arr[2]=3>1, include=1+0=1; skip=0; max=1. So include=2+1=3? Wait, trace: from i=0: include=1+recurse(1,1)=1+2=3 (since recurse(1,1) returns 2 as max length from index 1 with last=1, which is 2 from path 2→3). Skip=recurse(1,-inf)=2 (path 1→2→3). Max(3,2)=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Include or Skip Decision
ladders.desc: At each element, decide to include (if increasing) or skip, maximizing length.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves making a binary decision for each array element: include it in the current subsequence if it's greater than the last included element (ensuring increasing order), or skip it. This decision is based on maximizing the overall subsequence length. Intuition: by considering both possibilities, we explore all combinations to find the longest increasing path. Step-by-step: for element at index i with last value L, if arr[i] > L, we can include it, which adds 1 to length and updates L to arr[i]; otherwise, we must skip it. We then recursively apply this to the next index. The maximum length from include and skip choices gives the best outcome at this step. Time per decision is O(1), but overall leads to exponential exploration. Space is O(1) per call. This feeds into L1 as the decision-making inside the recursive function. Common mistakes: including when arr[i] <= L, which breaks increasing order; or not considering skip when include is invalid. Real code pattern: if arr[i] > last: include_option = 1 + solve(i+1, arr[i]); skip_option = solve(i+1, last); result = max(include_option, skip_option).

examples.trace: At i=0, last=3. arr[0]=4 > 3, so include is valid. Compute include_length = 1 + future from i=1 with last=4. Skip_length = future from i=1 with last=3. Since include adds to length, it's likely better, but we max later. For i=1: if include, arr[1]=5 > 4, include adds 1; skip doesn't. So include path gives length 2, skip gives 1. Max is include.

At i=0, last=7. arr[0]=6 > 7? no, so include invalid. Only skip is possible. Skip_length = future from i=1 with last=7. For i=1: arr[1]=2 > 7? no, include invalid; skip to end. So length 0. Decision is to skip.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Traverse a binary decision tree where each node represents include/skip choice.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves traversing a binary tree recursively, where each level corresponds to an array element, and each node has two children: include (if valid) and skip. The intuition is to model the problem as exploring all paths in this tree to find the path with maximum length under increasing constraints. At each node (index i, last value L), we generate children: if include is valid (arr[i] > L), create a child with updated last and increased length; always create a skip child with same last. We traverse depth-first to leaves (when i == n) and backtrack to compute max lengths. Step-by-step: start at root (i=0, L=-inf, length=0). For each node, recursively visit valid children, accumulating length. At leaf, return length; at internal node, return max of children's returns. Time: O(2^n) for full tree; space: O(n) for recursion depth. This feeds into L2 as the structural framework for decisions. Common mistakes: not pruning invalid include branches, or incorrect base case handling. Real code pattern: def traverse(i, last): if i==n: return 0; left = -inf; if arr[i]>last: left = 1 + traverse(i+1, arr[i]); right = traverse(i+1, last); return max(left, right).

examples.input: arr=[1,2], build tree from i=0, last=-inf
examples.trace: Root: i=0, last=-inf. Child include: valid since 1>-inf, go to i=1, last=1, length+1. From there: i=1, last=1. Include child: valid since 2>1, go to i=2 (leaf), length+1=2. Skip child: go to i=2, length=1. Max at i=1: max(2,1)=2. Back to root: skip child: i=1, last=-inf, length=0. From there: include: 2>-inf, length=1; skip: length=0; max=1. Root max: max(2,1)=2.

examples.input: arr=[3,1], build tree from i=0, last=-inf
examples.trace: Root: i=0, last=-inf. Include child: valid, i=1, last=3, length=1. From i=1: include invalid since 1>3? no; skip to leaf, length=1. So include path length=1. Skip child: i=1, last=-inf, length=0. From i=1: include valid since 1>-inf, length=1; skip length=0; max=1. Root max: max(1,1)=1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Increment
ladders.desc: Compare current element with last value, and increment length if increasing.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two actions: comparing the current array element to the last included value to check if it's greater (ensuring increasing order), and if so, incrementing the subsequence length by 1. The intuition is that an increasing subsequence grows only when we add an element larger than the previous one. Step-by-step: given last value L and current element arr[i], perform comparison: is arr[i] > L? If true, we can include arr[i], so new length = old length + 1, and update L to arr[i]. If false, length remains unchanged, and L stays the same. This operation is fundamental to building the subsequence. Time: O(1) per comparison; space: O(1). This feeds into L3 as the atomic step in tree traversal. Common mistakes: using >= instead of > (for strictly increasing), or not updating L correctly. Real code pattern: if arr[i] > last: length += 1; last = arr[i].

examples.trace: Compare: 8 > 5 is true. So include arr[i]. Increment length: 2 + 1 = 3. Update last to 8.
Compare: 3 > 10 is false. So cannot include arr[i]. Length remains 4. Last remains 10.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Subsequence Definition
ladders.desc: A subsequence is a sequence derived by deleting some elements without changing order.
ladders.explanation: A subsequence is a fundamental concept where we take a sequence (like an array) and create a new sequence by deleting zero or more elements, while keeping the remaining elements in their original order. Intuition: it's like picking a subset of positions from the original array, but the order must be preserved. For example, from [A, B, C], subsequences include [], [A], [B], [C], [A,B], [A,C], [B,C], [A,B,C]. Note that [C,A] is not a subsequence because order is changed. Step-by-step: given an array of length n, a subsequence can be represented by a binary mask of length n, where 1 means include and 0 means exclude. The number of subsequences is 2^n. Time to generate all is exponential. Space to store one is O(k) for length k. This feeds into L4 as we build subsequences by including/skipping elements. Common mistakes: confusing with substring (which requires contiguous elements) or subset (which ignores order). Real code pattern: to generate, iterate through indices, decide include/exclude. For LIS, we focus on increasing subsequences where elements are in order and increasing in value.

examples.trace: Start with empty []. Include index 0: [1]. Include index 1: [2]. Include both: [1,2]. No other combinations since order preserved.
List all: [] (none). Single elements: [3], [1], [4]. Pairs: [3,1] (indices 0,1), [3,4] (0,2), [1,4] (1,2). Triple: [3,1,4] (all). Total 2^3=8.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Memoization O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to store results of subproblems (LIS ending at each index), avoiding redundant calculations. This top-down DP approach improves over brute force by caching intermediate results.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Increasing Subsequence with Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using recursion with memoization to find LIS length
ladders.explanation: The Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) problem asks: given an array of integers, find the length of the longest subsequence where elements are strictly increasing. A subsequence maintains order but can skip elements. The dynamic programming with memoization approach solves this by breaking it into overlapping subproblems: for each index i, we compute LIS ending at i (lis_ending_at[i]), meaning the longest increasing subsequence that must include nums[i] as its last element. The recurrence relation is: lis_ending_at[i] = 1 + max(lis_ending_at[j]) for all j < i where nums[j] < nums[i], or 1 if no such j exists. The overall LIS is max(lis_ending_at[i]) across all i. Memoization stores computed lis_ending_at[i] values to avoid redundant calculations in recursive calls. Intuitively, we explore all possible ending points and build upon smaller subsequences. The algorithm uses a recursive function lis_ending_at(i) that returns the LIS length ending at index i, with memoization via a dictionary or array. Base case: if i == 0, return 1 (single element). Recursive case: initialize max_length = 1, iterate j from 0 to i-1, if nums[j] < nums[i], recursively compute lis_ending_at(j) and update max_length = max(max_length, 1 + lis_ending_at(j)). Store result in memo[i] and return. Finally, iterate i from 0 to n-1, compute lis_ending_at(i), and track maximum. Time complexity is O(n²) due to nested loops in recursion (each lis_ending_at(i) may check all previous j), and space complexity is O(n²) in worst-case recursion depth and memo storage, but typically O(n) for memo array. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of computing LIS ending at each index. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle empty arrays (return 0), not using strict increasing condition, or incorrect base cases. Real code pattern: def lis_memo(nums): n = len(nums); memo = [-1]*n; def lis_ending_at(i): if memo[i] != -1: return memo[i]; max_len = 1; for j in range(i): if nums[j] < nums[i]: max_len = max(max_len, 1 + lis_ending_at(j)); memo[i] = max_len; return max_len; if n == 0: return 0; result = 0; for i in range(n): result = max(result, lis_ending_at(i)); return result.

examples.trace: nums = [10,9,2,5,3,7,101,18], n=8. Initialize memo = [-1]*8. Call lis_ending_at(0): j loop none, max_len=1, memo[0]=1, return 1. lis_ending_at(1): j=0, nums[0]=10 >=9, max_len=1, memo[1]=1, return 1. lis_ending_at(2): j=0,10>=2; j=1,9>=2; max_len=1, memo[2]=1, return 1. lis_ending_at(3): j=0,10>=5; j=1,9>=5; j=2,2<5, call lis_ending_at(2)=1, max_len=max(1,1+1)=2; memo[3]=2, return 2. lis_ending_at(4): j=0,10>=3; j=1,9>=3; j=2,2<3, call lis_ending_at(2)=1, max_len=2; j=3,5>=3; memo[4]=2, return 2. lis_ending_at(5): j=0,10>=7; j=1,9>=7; j=2,2<7, call lis_ending_at(2)=1, max_len=2; j=3,5<7, call lis_ending_at(3)=2, max_len=max(2,1+2)=3; j=4,3<7, call lis_ending_at(4)=2, max_len=max(3,1+2)=3; memo[5]=3, return 3. lis_ending_at(6): j=0,10<101, call lis_ending_at(0)=1, max_len=2; j=1,9<101, call lis_ending_at(1)=1, max_len=2; j=2,2<101, call lis_ending_at(2)=1, max_len=2; j=3,5<101, call lis_ending_at(3)=2, max_len=3; j=4,3<101, call lis_ending_at(4)=2, max_len=3; j=5,7<101, call lis_ending_at(5)=3, max_len=4; memo[6]=4, return 4. lis_ending_at(7): j=0,10<18, call lis_ending_at(0)=1, max_len=2; j=1,9<18, call lis_ending_at(1)=1, max_len=2; j=2,2<18, call lis_ending_at(2)=1, max_len=2; j=3,5<18, call lis_ending_at(3)=2, max_len=3; j=4,3<18, call lis_ending_at(4)=2, max_len=3; j=5,7<18, call lis_ending_at(5)=3, max_len=4; j=6,101>=18; memo[7]=4, return 4. Overall max = max(1,1,1,2,2,3,4,4) = 4.

nums = [0,1,0,3,2,3], n=6. memo = [-1]*6. lis_ending_at(0): max_len=1, memo[0]=1, return 1. lis_ending_at(1): j=0,0<1, call lis_ending_at(0)=1, max_len=2; memo[1]=2, return 2. lis_ending_at(2): j=0,0>=0; j=1,1>=0; max_len=1, memo[2]=1, return 1. lis_ending_at(3): j=0,0<3, call lis_ending_at(0)=1, max_len=2; j=1,1<3, call lis_ending_at(1)=2, max_len=3; j=2,0<3, call lis_ending_at(2)=1, max_len=3; memo[3]=3, return 3. lis_ending_at(4): j=0,0<2, call lis_ending_at(0)=1, max_len=2; j=1,1<2, call lis_ending_at(1)=2, max_len=3; j=2,0<2, call lis_ending_at(2)=1, max_len=3; j=3,3>=2; memo[4]=3, return 3. lis_ending_at(5): j=0,0<3, call lis_ending_at(0)=1, max_len=2; j=1,1<3, call lis_ending_at(1)=2, max_len=3; j=2,0<3, call lis_ending_at(2)=1, max_len=3; j=3,3>=3; j=4,2<3, call lis_ending_at(4)=3, max_len=4; memo[5]=4, return 4. Overall max = max(1,2,1,3,3,4) = 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute LIS Ending at Index i
ladders.desc: Recursive function with memoization to find LIS length ending at a specific index
ladders.explanation: This subroutine computes lis_ending_at(i): the length of the longest increasing subsequence that must end with element nums[i]. It is the core building block for the full LIS solution. Intuition: To find LIS ending at i, we look at all previous indices j < i where nums[j] < nums[i]. For each such j, we can extend the LIS ending at j by appending nums[i], giving length 1 + lis_ending_at(j). We take the maximum over all j, or 1 if no such j exists (i.e., nums[i] starts a new subsequence). Memoization stores computed results to avoid recomputation. Step-by-step: Define function lis_ending_at(i) with memo array memo of size n initialized to -1. Base case: if i == 0, return 1 (though handled in recursion). Recursive case: if memo[i] != -1, return memo[i]. Initialize max_len = 1. For j from 0 to i-1: if nums[j] < nums[i], recursively compute lis_ending_at(j) and update max_len = max(max_len, 1 + lis_ending_at(j)). Set memo[i] = max_len and return. Time complexity for one call is O(i) in worst case, but with memoization across calls, overall O(n²). Space complexity is O(n) for memo array and recursion stack. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each i to compute the full LIS. Common mistakes: not checking j < i condition properly, forgetting to add 1 when extending, or incorrect base case handling. Real code pattern: def lis_ending_at(i, nums, memo): if memo[i] != -1: return memo[i]; max_len = 1; for j in range(i): if nums[j] < nums[i]: max_len = max(max_len, 1 + lis_ending_at(j, nums, memo)); memo[i] = max_len; return max_len.

examples.trace: nums = [1,2,3], i=2, memo = [-1,-1,-1]. Call lis_ending_at(2): j=0, nums[0]=1<3, call lis_ending_at(0): memo[0]=-1, max_len=1 (no j since i=0), memo[0]=1, return 1. max_len = max(1,1+1)=2. j=1, nums[1]=2<3, call lis_ending_at(1): memo[1]=-1, j=0, nums[0]=1<2, call lis_ending_at(0)=1, max_len=2, memo[1]=2, return 2. max_len = max(2,1+2)=3. memo[2]=3, return 3.
nums = [5,4,3], i=2, memo = [-1,-1,-1]. Call lis_ending_at(2): j=0, nums[0]=5>=3; j=1, nums[1]=4>=3; max_len=1, memo[2]=1, return 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recurrence Relation for LIS Ending at i
ladders.desc: Derive and apply the recurrence: lis_ending_at(i) = 1 + max(lis_ending_at(j)) for j < i and nums[j] < nums[i]
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the recurrence relation that defines lis_ending_at(i) in terms of smaller subproblems. Intuition: The LIS ending at i can be formed by taking any LIS ending at a previous index j where nums[j] < nums[i], and appending nums[i]. Thus, its length is 1 plus the length of that LIS ending at j. We maximize over all valid j to find the longest possible. If no such j exists, lis_ending_at(i) = 1 (the subsequence containing only nums[i]). This recurrence captures optimal substructure: the optimal solution for i uses optimal solutions for j. Step-by-step: Given array nums of length n, for each i from 0 to n-1, compute lis[i] using: Initialize lis[i] = 1. For j from 0 to i-1: if nums[j] < nums[i], then lis[i] = max(lis[i], 1 + lis[j]). This is the bottom-up version; in memoization, we compute recursively. Time analysis: Computing lis[i] takes O(i) time, leading to O(n²) total. Space: O(n) for lis array. Connection to L1: This recurrence is implemented in the lis_ending_at function. Common mistakes: Using ≤ instead of < for increasing (should be strict), or not adding 1 when extending. Real code pattern: for i in range(n): lis[i] = 1; for j in range(i): if nums[j] < nums[i]: lis[i] = max(lis[i], lis[j] + 1).

ladders.title: Building Block — Memoization in Recursion
ladders.desc: Implement memoization to cache results of recursive calls and avoid recomputation
ladders.explanation: Memoization is an optimization technique where we store the results of expensive function calls and return the cached result when the same inputs occur again. In the context of LIS, we use it to store lis_ending_at(i) values so that recursive calls for the same i are computed only once. Intuition: The recurrence for lis_ending_at(i) involves overlapping subproblems; e.g., lis_ending_at(3) may call lis_ending_at(1), and lis_ending_at(4) may also call lis_ending_at(1). Without memoization, this leads to exponential time; with memoization, it reduces to O(n²). Step-by-step: Create an array memo of size n initialized with a sentinel value (e.g., -1) indicating not computed. In the recursive function, first check if memo[i] != sentinel; if yes, return memo[i]. Otherwise, compute the result as described, store it in memo[i], and return it. This ensures each subproblem is solved once. Time analysis: Each lis_ending_at(i) is computed once, and each computation involves O(i) work, so total O(n²). Space: O(n) for memo array plus O(n) recursion stack in worst case. Connection to L2: Memoization is applied to the recurrence relation to make it efficient. Common mistakes: Forgetting to initialize memo properly, using a mutable default argument in Python, or not checking memo before recursion. Real code pattern: memo = [-1]*n; def lis_ending_at(i): if memo[i] != -1: return memo[i]; # compute result; memo[i] = result; return result.

examples.trace: n=3, memo = [-1,-1,-1]. Call lis_ending_at(2): memo[2]==-1, compute. j=0, nums[0]=1<3, call lis_ending_at(0): memo[0]==-1, compute: max_len=1, memo[0]=1, return 1. max_len=2. j=1, nums[1]=2<3, call lis_ending_at(1): memo[1]==-1, compute: j=0, nums[0]=1<2, call lis_ending_at(0)=1 (from memo), max_len=2, memo[1]=2, return 2. max_len=3. memo[2]=3, return 3. Final memo = [1,2,3].

examples.input: nums = [3, 1, 2], compute lis_ending_at(0), lis_ending_at(1), lis_ending_at(2) with memo
examples.trace: n=3, memo = [-1,-1,-1]. lis_ending_at(0): memo[0]==-1, max_len=1, memo[0]=1, return 1. lis_ending_at(1): memo[1]==-1, j=0, nums[0]=3>=1, max_len=1, memo[1]=1, return 1. lis_ending_at(2): memo[2]==-1, j=0, nums[0]=3>=2; j=1, nums[1]=1<2, call lis_ending_at(1)=1 (from memo), max_len=2, memo[2]=2, return 2. Final memo = [1,1,2].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Extend Subsequence
ladders.desc: Check if an element can extend a subsequence and update length accordingly
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two steps: (1) Compare two elements nums[j] and nums[i] to see if nums[j] < nums[i] (strictly increasing condition). (2) If true, extend the subsequence ending at j by appending nums[i], which increases its length by 1. This operation is used in the inner loop of the LIS recurrence. Intuition: To build an increasing subsequence, each new element must be greater than the last element in the subsequence. So when considering nums[i] as a potential new end, we check all previous nums[j]; if nums[j] is smaller, we can place nums[i] after it, forming a longer subsequence. The update is: new_length = current_length_ending_at_j + 1. We keep track of the maximum such new_length for each i. Step-by-step: Given indices j and i with j < i, if nums[j] < nums[i], then potential_length = 1 + length_ending_at_j. Compare potential_length with current best length for i, and update if larger. Time analysis: Each comparison is O(1), and we do O(n²) comparisons total. Space: O(1) per operation. Connection to L3: This operation is performed within memoized recursive calls. Common mistakes: Using ≤ instead of <, or forgetting to add 1 when extending. Real code pattern: if nums[j] < nums[i]: max_len = max(max_len, 1 + lis_ending_at_j).

examples.trace: nums[0]=1, nums[2]=2. Compare: 1 < 2 is true. Extend: potential_length = 1 + length_ending_at_j = 1 + 1 = 2. So nums[2] can extend the subsequence ending at nums[0] to length 2.
nums[1]=4, nums[2]=3. Compare: 4 < 3 is false. So nums[2] cannot extend the subsequence ending at nums[1]. Potential length not calculated.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Subsequence and Increasing Order
ladders.desc: Understand what a subsequence is and the definition of strictly increasing order
ladders.explanation: A subsequence of a sequence is obtained by deleting zero or more elements without changing the order of the remaining elements. For example, from [A, B, C, D], subsequences include [A, C], [B, D], [A, B, C], etc. The empty sequence [] is also a subsequence. Importantly, a subsequence does not need to be contiguous; it can skip elements. An increasing subsequence is one where each element is strictly greater than the previous element. Formally, for a subsequence [a_i1, a_i2, ..., a_ik] with indices i1 < i2 < ... < ik, it is increasing if a_i1 < a_i2 < ... < a_ik. 'Strictly' means no equal elements allowed. The Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) problem asks for the maximum length of such a subsequence. Intuition: We want to pick elements in order such that each picked element is larger than the last, and we maximize the count. This foundational concept is crucial because the DP approach builds subsequences ending at each index. Step-by-step: Given an array, identify subsequences by selecting indices in increasing order. Check if selected elements are in increasing order. Find the longest such. Time analysis: Brute force would check all 2^n subsequences. Space: O(1) for concept. Connection to L4: This defines the condition for extending a subsequence. Common mistakes: Confusing subsequence with substring (which is contiguous), or allowing non-strict increasing. Real code pattern: No code, but understanding: for i in range(n): for j in range(i+1, n): if nums[j] > nums[i], can extend.

examples.trace: Subsequences: [], [3], [1], [2], [3,1], [3,2], [1,2], [3,1,2]. Increasing: [3], [1], [2], [1,2]. Longest is [1,2] with length 2 (or [3] but shorter).

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Tabulation O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Build a DP table iteratively where each entry represents the LIS ending at that index, using previous results to compute current values. This bottom-up approach efficiently solves subproblems in order.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Increasing Subsequence with DP Tabulation
ladders.desc: Complete O(n²) solution using dynamic programming tabulation to find the longest increasing subsequence.
ladders.explanation: The Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) problem asks: given an array of integers, find the length of the longest subsequence where elements are strictly increasing in order (not necessarily contiguous). The DP tabulation approach solves this by building a DP table where dp[i] represents the length of the longest increasing subsequence ending at index i. The intuition is that to compute dp[i], we look at all previous indices j < i where nums[j] < nums[i], and take the maximum dp[j] value, then add 1 for the current element. This works because any increasing subsequence ending at i must extend some increasing subsequence ending at a smaller element before i. We initialize all dp[i] = 1 since each element alone forms a subsequence of length 1. The algorithm iterates through the array: for each i from 0 to n-1, for each j from 0 to i-1, if nums[j] < nums[i], update dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1). Finally, the answer is max(dp). Time complexity is O(n²) due to nested loops, and space is O(n) for the dp array. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete solution that uses the key subroutine of comparing and updating dp values. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize dp values to 1, incorrectly handling equal elements (should be strictly increasing), or returning dp[n-1] instead of max(dp). Real code pattern: def lis(nums): n = len(nums); dp = [1]*n; for i in range(n): for j in range(i): if nums[j] < nums[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j]+1); return max(dp).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Value Update for Current Index
ladders.desc: Compute dp[i] by checking all previous indices and updating based on increasing condition.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the DP tabulation approach: given an array nums and a DP array dp where dp[k] stores the LIS length ending at index k, compute dp[i] for a specific index i. The intuition is that to find the longest increasing subsequence ending at i, we must consider all previous indices j where nums[j] < nums[i], because we can extend any increasing subsequence ending at j by appending nums[i]. We initialize dp[i] = 1 (the subsequence containing only nums[i]), then iterate j from 0 to i-1: if nums[j] < nums[i], we update dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1). This ensures dp[i] captures the maximum length possible. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Set dp[i] = 1. 2. For j in range(0, i): if nums[j] < nums[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1). Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(i) which in the full problem sums to O(n²), and space is O(1) extra beyond input. This connects to L0 by being the inner loop that builds the DP table. Common mistakes include using <= instead of < for strictly increasing, or not initializing dp[i] to 1. Real code pattern: def update_dp(i, nums, dp): dp[i] = 1; for j in range(i): if nums[j] < nums[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Extending Subsequences from Previous Elements
ladders.desc: The insight that LIS ending at i can be built by extending LIS from smaller previous elements.
ladders.explanation: This core logic captures the fundamental idea behind the DP solution: for an increasing subsequence ending at index i, it must be an extension of some increasing subsequence ending at a previous index j where nums[j] < nums[i]. Therefore, the length of LIS ending at i is 1 plus the maximum length of LIS ending at any j < i with nums[j] < nums[i]. If no such j exists, the LIS ending at i is just the element itself (length 1). The intuition is recursive: LIS(i) = 1 + max(LIS(j)) for all j < i with nums[j] < nums[i]. This is a optimal substructure property—the solution to a larger problem depends on solutions to smaller subproblems. In tabulation, we compute this iteratively from left to right. Step-by-step: For each i, look at all j from 0 to i-1, check if nums[j] < nums[i], and keep track of the maximum dp[j]. Then dp[i] = that max + 1, or 1 if no j satisfies. Time analysis for this logic applied to one i is O(i), space is O(1). This connects to L1 as the reasoning behind the update rule. Common mistakes include misunderstanding 'subsequence' vs 'subarray', or not handling the base case of length 1. Real code pattern: max_len = 1; for j in range(i): if nums[j] < nums[i]: max_len = max(max_len, dp[j] + 1); dp[i] = max_len.

examples.output: LIS ending at index 6 is length 4
examples.trace: For i=6 (nums[6]=101). Consider j from 0 to 5: j=0, nums[0]=10<101, LIS length from j=0 is dp[0]=1, candidate length=2; j=1, nums[1]=9<101, dp[1]=1, candidate=2; j=2, nums[2]=2<101, dp[2]=1, candidate=2; j=3, nums[3]=5<101, dp[3]=2, candidate=3; j=4, nums[4]=3<101, dp[4]=2, candidate=3; j=5, nums[5]=7<101, dp[5]=3, candidate=4. Maximum candidate is 4, so LIS ending at index 6 has length 4.

examples.output: LIS ending at index 5 is length 4
examples.trace: For i=5 (nums[5]=3). Consider j from 0 to 4: j=0, nums[0]=0<3, dp[0]=1, candidate=2; j=1, nums[1]=1<3, dp[1]=2, candidate=3; j=2, nums[2]=0<3, dp[2]=1, candidate=2; j=3, nums[3]=3>=3, skip; j=4, nums[4]=2<3, dp[4]=3, candidate=4. Maximum candidate is 4, so LIS ending at index 5 has length 4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating and Comparing Array Elements
ladders.desc: Loop through indices and compare values to find smaller previous elements.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the basic operation of iterating through an array and comparing elements to identify pairs where a previous element is smaller than a current element. Specifically, for a given index i in an array nums, we need to check all indices j from 0 to i-1 and determine if nums[j] < nums[i]. This is essential for the DP approach because it identifies which previous subsequences can be extended. The intuition is simple: we scan the left portion of the array to find candidates that can precede nums[i] in an increasing subsequence. Step-by-step: 1. Set current value = nums[i]. 2. For j in range(0, i): if nums[j] < current, then j is a valid predecessor. We can collect such j or count them. Time complexity is O(i), space is O(1). This connects to L2 as the mechanism to implement the core logic of extending from smaller elements. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop ranges or incorrect comparison operators. Real code pattern: def find_smaller_predecessors(i, nums): predecessors = []; for j in range(i): if nums[j] < nums[i]: predecessors.append(j); return predecessors.

examples.trace: For i=5, nums[5]=7. j=0: nums[0]=10 >=7, skip. j=1: nums[1]=9 >=7, skip. j=2: nums[2]=2 <7, include j=2. j=3: nums[3]=5 <7, include j=3. j=4: nums[4]=3 <7, include j=4. Result list: [2,3,4].
For i=4, nums[4]=2. j=0: nums[0]=0 <2, include j=0. j=1: nums[1]=1 <2, include j=1. j=2: nums[2]=0 <2, include j=2. j=3: nums[3]=3 >=2, skip. Result list: [0,1,2].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Comparing Two Array Elements
ladders.desc: Retrieve values from an array by index and compare them using relational operators.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two fundamental actions: accessing an element in an array using its index, and comparing two numerical values to determine if one is less than the other. In programming, array access is typically O(1) time, and comparison is a constant-time operation. For the LIS problem, we need to compare nums[j] and nums[i] to check if nums[j] < nums[i], which is the condition for an increasing subsequence. The intuition is straightforward: we look at the values at specific positions and use the less-than operator to see if the earlier value is smaller. Step-by-step: 1. Given array nums and indices j and i. 2. Retrieve value_a = nums[j] and value_b = nums[i]. 3. Evaluate the expression value_a < value_b, which returns True or False. Time and space are O(1). This connects to L3 as the atomic step within the iteration loop. Common mistakes include using wrong indices (e.g., off-by-one) or confusing comparison operators (e.g., using <= instead of <). Real code pattern: def compare_elements(nums, j, i): return nums[j] < nums[i].

examples.trace: Access nums[2] = 2. Access nums[5] = 7. Compare 2 < 7: True.
Access nums[3] = 3. Access nums[4] = 2. Compare 3 < 2: False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understanding arrays as ordered collections and accessing elements by position.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers arrays (or lists in Python), which are data structures that store elements in a sequential order. Each element has an index, starting from 0 for the first element, 1 for the second, and so on. Accessing an element by its index allows retrieval of its value in constant time. For example, if arr = [10, 20, 30], then arr[0] = 10, arr[1] = 20, arr[2] = 30. The intuition is that arrays provide fast, random access to elements based on their position. This is crucial for the LIS problem because we need to compare elements at specific indices. Step-by-step: 1. Define an array with elements. 2. Use index notation (e.g., arr[i]) to get the value at position i. 3. Ensure indices are within bounds (0 to length-1). Time and space are O(1) for access. This connects to L4 as the prerequisite for accessing elements before comparing them. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing or accessing out-of-bounds indices. Real code pattern: arr = [3, 1, 4]; first_element = arr[0]  # equals 3.

examples.trace: Array has 8 elements. Index 3 refers to the fourth element (since indexing starts at 0). Access arr[3]: value is 5.
Array has 6 elements. Index 5 refers to the sixth element. Access arr[5]: value is 3.

approaches.name: Patience Sorting with Binary Search O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Maintain an array of the smallest possible tails for increasing subsequences of various lengths, using binary search to update efficiently. This method simulates a card game to find the LIS length optimally.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Increasing Subsequence using Patience Sorting
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of LIS using patience sorting with binary search in O(n log n) time
ladders.explanation: The Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) problem asks: given an array of integers, find the length of the longest subsequence where elements are strictly increasing. A subsequence maintains order but not necessarily consecutive elements. The patience sorting approach simulates a card game: we maintain piles of cards where each pile's top card is the smallest possible ending value for a subsequence of that pile's length. As we process each number, we find the leftmost pile where it can be placed (using binary search), creating a new pile if it's larger than all existing top cards. The number of piles at the end equals the LIS length. Intuition: each pile represents a potential increasing subsequence, and by always placing cards on the leftmost valid pile, we ensure we're building the most 'optimistic' sequences possible. The algorithm: 1. Initialize empty array 'tails' to store pile tops. 2. For each number x in input: a. Binary search tails for first element ≥ x. b. If found, replace it with x. c. Else, append x to tails. 3. Return length of tails. Time: O(n log n) for n elements with binary search. Space: O(n) for tails array. Connection: This is the complete solution using all building blocks. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle empty input, using ≤ instead of < for strictly increasing, not understanding why this gives length but not the actual subsequence. Real pattern: tails = []; for x in nums: pos = bisect_left(tails, x); if pos == len(tails): tails.append(x); else: tails[pos] = x; return len(tails).

examples.trace:
Input: [10,9,2,5,3,7,101,18]
Step 1: x=10, tails=[], binary search finds position 0 (not found), append: tails=[10]
Step 2: x=9, tails=[10], binary search for first ≥9 finds position 0 (10≥9), replace: tails=[9]
Step 3: x=2, tails=[9], binary search for first ≥2 finds position 0 (9≥2), replace: tails=[2]
Step 4: x=5, tails=[2], binary search for first ≥5 finds position 1 (not found), append: tails=[2,5]
Step 5: x=3, tails=[2,5], binary search for first ≥3 finds position 1 (5≥3), replace: tails=[2,3]
Step 6: x=7, tails=[2,3], binary search for first ≥7 finds position 2 (not found), append: tails=[2,3,7]
Step 7: x=101, tails=[2,3,7], binary search for first ≥101 finds position 3 (not found), append: tails=[2,3,7,101]
Step 8: x=18, tails=[2,3,7,101], binary search for first ≥18 finds position 3 (101≥18), replace: tails=[2,3,7,18]
Final tails length = 4, LIS length = 4 (e.g., [2,3,7,101] or [2,5,7,101])

Input: [0,1,0,3,2,3]
Step 1: x=0, tails=[], append: tails=[0]
Step 2: x=1, tails=[0], binary search for first ≥1 finds position 1 (not found), append: tails=[0,1]
Step 3: x=0, tails=[0,1], binary search for first ≥0 finds position 0 (0≥0), replace: tails=[0,1]
Step 4: x=3, tails=[0,1], binary search for first ≥3 finds position 2 (not found), append: tails=[0,1,3]
Step 5: x=2, tails=[0,1,3], binary search for first ≥2 finds position 2 (3≥2), replace: tails=[0,1,2]
Step 6: x=3, tails=[0,1,2], binary search for first ≥3 finds position 3 (not found), append: tails=[0,1,2,3]
Final tails length = 4, LIS length = 4 (e.g., [0,1,2,3])

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search in Sorted Array for Insertion Position
ladders.desc: Find the position to insert an element in a sorted array using binary search
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the patience sorting algorithm. Given a sorted array and a target value, we need to find the leftmost position where we could insert the target while maintaining sorted order. Specifically, we want the first position where array[pos] ≥ target (for strictly increasing LIS). This is different from standard binary search that looks for exact matches - here we're finding an insertion point. Intuition: binary search efficiently halves the search space by comparing middle element with target. If middle ≥ target, we search left half (including middle since it might be the first ≥). If middle < target, we search right half (excluding middle). We maintain 'left' and 'right' pointers that converge to the insertion point. Algorithm: 1. Initialize left=0, right=len(array). 2. While left < right: a. mid = (left+right)//2. b. If array[mid] ≥ target: right = mid. c. Else: left = mid+1. 3. Return left. Time: O(log n) where n is array length. Space: O(1). Connection: This feeds into L0 by efficiently finding where to place each new card in the patience sorting piles. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in bounds, using > instead of ≥, not handling empty array case. Real pattern: def bisect_left(arr, x): left, right = 0, len(arr); while left < right: mid = (left+right)//2; if arr[mid] >= x: right = mid; else: left = mid+1; return left.

examples.trace:
Array: [2,4,6,8], target=5
Step 1: left=0, right=4, mid=2, arr[2]=6≥5 → right=2
Step 2: left=0, right=2, mid=1, arr[1]=4<5 → left=2
Step 3: left=2, right=2, loop ends
Return 2 (insert 5 at index 2: [2,4,5,6,8])

Array: [1,3,5], target=0
Step 1: left=0, right=3, mid=1, arr[1]=3≥0 → right=1
Step 2: left=0, right=1, mid=0, arr[0]=1≥0 → right=0
Step 3: left=0, right=0, loop ends
Return 0 (insert 0 at index 0: [0,1,3,5])

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Smallest Possible Tails for Each Length
ladders.desc: The key insight: track the smallest possible ending value for increasing subsequences of each length
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique that makes patience sorting work for LIS. Instead of tracking actual subsequences (which would be exponential), we maintain an array 'tails' where tails[i] = the smallest possible ending value of any increasing subsequence of length i+1. Why does this work? Intuition: For building longer subsequences, we want ending values to be as small as possible to give future numbers more opportunities to extend them. When we process a new number x: 1. If x is larger than all tails, it can extend the longest subsequence, so we append it (creating a new length). 2. Otherwise, we find where x fits and update that position - this doesn't change the length count but makes future extensions more likely. Critical insight: tails array is always strictly increasing. Proof: if tails[i] ≥ tails[i+1], then the subsequence ending with tails[i+1] (length i+2) has a smaller ending than one of length i+1, contradiction. This monotonic property enables binary search. Algorithm outline: Start with empty tails. For each x: find position where x should go in tails (first element ≥ x). If position exists, replace with x (making that length's ending smaller). Else append x. Time per element: O(log n) for search. Space: O(n). Connection: This logic uses L1's binary search and feeds into L0. Common mistakes: not understanding why tails tracks smallest endings, thinking tails contains the actual LIS. Real pattern: tails maintained as sorted array; each update preserves the 'smallest possible ending' invariant.

examples.trace:
Step 1: x=3, tails=[], append: tails=[3] (length 1 ends with 3)
Step 2: x=1, tails=[3], find first ≥1: position 0, replace: tails=[1] (length 1 now ends with 1 - smaller!)
Step 3: x=4, tails=[1], find first ≥4: position 1 (not found), append: tails=[1,4] (length 1 ends with 1, length 2 ends with 4)
Step 4: x=2, tails=[1,4], find first ≥2: position 1 (4≥2), replace: tails=[1,2] (length 2 now ends with 2 - smaller!)
Final tails=[1,2], length=2, LIS length=2

Step 1: x=5, tails=[], append: tails=[5]
Step 2: x=6, tails=[5], append: tails=[5,6]
Step 3: x=1, tails=[5,6], find first ≥1: position 0, replace: tails=[1,6]
Step 4: x=2, tails=[1,6], find first ≥2: position 1 (6≥2), replace: tails=[1,2]
Step 5: x=3, tails=[1,2], append: tails=[1,2,3]
Final tails length=3, LIS length=3

ladders.title: Building Block — Understanding Subsequences vs Subarrays
ladders.desc: Distinguish between subsequences (maintaining order) and subarrays (consecutive elements)
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is crucial for LIS. A subsequence is a sequence derived by deleting some or no elements without changing the order of remaining elements. Contrast with subarray which must be consecutive. For array [a,b,c,d]: subsequences include [a,c], [b,d], [a,b,c]; subarrays include [a,b], [b,c,d]. Key properties: 1. Number of subsequences is 2^n (each element can be included or not). 2. Order is preserved. 3. Not necessarily contiguous. For LIS, we seek the longest subsequence where elements are strictly increasing. Example: [3,1,4,2] has LIS length 2 ([1,4] or [3,4] or [1,2]). Why subsequences matter: they give more flexibility than subarrays, making the problem harder but also enabling optimizations like patience sorting. Intuition: we can 'skip' elements that don't fit the increasing pattern. Connection to patience sorting: when we place a card on a pile, we're effectively choosing which previous elements to include in that subsequence. Common mistakes: confusing with longest increasing subarray, not understanding that subsequences maintain order. Real pattern: think of subsequence as a selection mask over the original array.

examples.trace:
All subarrays (consecutive): [1], [2], [3], [4], [1,2], [2,3], [3,4], [1,2,3], [2,3,4], [1,2,3,4]
Subsequences (non-consecutive allowed): all subarrays plus [1,3], [1,4], [2,4], [1,2,4], [1,3,4], etc.
LIS: [1,2,3,4] (length 4) - happens to be a subarray here but isn't always

Array: [5,1,2,3]
Longest increasing subarray (consecutive): [1,2,3] length 3
Longest increasing subsequence: [1,2,3] also length 3 (but note we skipped element 5)
If array was [5,1,3,2,4]: LIS would be [1,3,4] or [1,2,4] length 3, while longest increasing subarray is only [1,3] or [2,4] length 2

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and Replacing Elements in Array
ladders.desc: Fundamental skill: compare values and conditionally update array elements
ladders.explanation: This basic operation underpins the patience sorting algorithm. At its core, we need to: 1. Compare a new value with existing array elements. 2. Based on comparison, either replace an element or append to the array. Intuition: arrays are indexed collections; we can access, compare, and modify elements by position. For patience sorting: we compare new number x with tails[pos] (found via binary search). If pos is within bounds (pos < len(tails)), we replace tails[pos] = x. Otherwise (pos == len(tails)), we append x to tails. Key details: array indices start at 0; append increases length by 1; replacement doesn't change length. Time: O(1) for access/replace/append. Space: O(1) for the operation (though array may need resizing). Connection: this feeds into L2's maintenance of tails array. Common mistakes: index out of bounds, confusing replacement with insertion (which shifts elements), not handling empty array case. Real pattern: if pos < len(arr): arr[pos] = x; else: arr.append(x).

examples.trace:
Initial array: [2,4,6], length=3
pos=1 is within bounds (1 < 3)
Compare: arr[1]=4, x=3
Since we're doing patience sorting logic: replace arr[1] with 3
Result: [2,3,6]
Length remains 3

Initial array: [1,3], length=2
pos=2 equals length (2 == 2)
This means position is at end
Append x=5 to array
Result: [1,3,5]
Length becomes 3

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is an Increasing Sequence?
ladders.desc: Most basic prerequisite: understanding strictly increasing order of numbers
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for LIS. A sequence of numbers is strictly increasing if each element is greater than the previous one. Formally: for sequence a1, a2, ..., ak, we require a1 < a2 < ... < ak. Examples: [1,2,3] is increasing; [1,1,2] is NOT strictly increasing (has equality); [3,2,1] is decreasing; [1,3,2] is not increasing (3>1 but 2<3). Key points: 1. 'Strictly' means no equal elements allowed (use ≤ for non-decreasing). 2. Order matters - check each adjacent pair. 3. Length 1 sequence is trivially increasing. Why this matters for LIS: we're looking for the longest subsequence with this property. Intuition: increasing sequences naturally arise in sorted data, growth patterns, etc. Connection: this defines the constraint for L0 problem. Common mistakes: confusing with non-decreasing, not checking all pairs. Real pattern: for i from 0 to len(seq)-2: if seq[i] >= seq[i+1]: not strictly increasing.

examples.trace:
Check pairs: 1<2 ✓, 2<3 ✓, 3<4 ✓
All pairs satisfy < relation
Therefore strictly increasing

Check pairs: 5<3 ✗ (5 is not less than 3)
Fails at first pair
Even though 3<7 and 7>2, the sequence is not increasing overall
